title: SEGMENT 18 — The future: Revio, the cost-down trajectory, PacBio in clinical genomics

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The future: Revio, the cost-down trajectory, PacBio in clinical genomics
Let me close with a segment on where PacBio is headed, and what to watch for over the next five to ten years.
The dominant trend, as with every sequencing platform in history, is cost reduction.
PacBio HiFi today, on a Revio, generates a human genome for somewhere in the low hundreds of dollars in reagent cost.
That's already a huge drop from where the platform was just three years ago.

PacBio has publicly stated targets to drive this cost down further over the next few instrument generations, with the goal of bringing HiFi human genomes into clinical-realistic price ranges — comparable to what Illumina charges today.

If that happens — and it's plausible based on the historical trajectory — then PacBio HiFi becomes a real candidate for first-line clinical genome sequencing in cases where the extra information (structural variants, methylation, phasing) is clinically actionable.
We're already seeing this in rare disease diagnostics, where HiFi has produced diagnoses in cases that Illumina missed.
As the cost gap narrows, the calculus shifts further in PacBio's favor for clinical applications.
Throughput growth is the second trend.
Each PacBio instrument generation has roughly an order-of-magnitude jump in throughput.
The Sequel II's eight million ZMWs were eight times the Sequel; the Revio's twenty-five million ZMWs (per cell, with four cells per run) are several times the Sequel II.

If this pace continues, the next-generation instrument should be in the hundreds-of-millions-of-ZMWs range, with throughput per instrument approaching what current Illumina NovaSeq instruments deliver.
PacBio is investing heavily in nanofabrication to make this happen.
New acquisitions and product expansions.
PacBio acquired Omniome in 2021 — Omniome had developed a different sequencing chemistry, based on the concept of "sequencing by binding," which they were pursuing for short-read applications.
PacBio also acquired Apton Biosystems in 2023, which had developed proteomics-related single-molecule imaging.

The pattern suggests PacBio is positioning to be a multi-modality single-molecule company — long-read DNA, short-read DNA, perhaps eventually direct protein or RNA detection — rather than purely a long-read DNA sequencer.
Clinical genomics adoption is the most important strategic battle ahead.
PacBio is actively pursuing clinical lab partnerships, regulatory approvals (their Revio has been validated in clinical settings), and integration with clinical pipelines.

The pitch to clinical genomics is essentially: "For rare disease, for cancer, for any case where structural variation or comprehensive variant detection matters, you need long reads — and HiFi gives you the accuracy you need for clinical decision making.
" Whether this pitch lands at scale depends on cost reduction, on payer reimbursement, and on accumulating clinical evidence that HiFi adds real value over standard Illumina workflows.
The competitive dynamic with Nanopore remains live.
Nanopore is also chasing clinical genomics, with somewhat different angles — portability, rapid turnaround, in-situ deployment for things like pathogen identification.
Both platforms will probably find clinical niches.
The two-platform duopoly within long reads seems stable for the foreseeable future.
The competitive dynamic with Illumina is fascinating.
Illumina has its own short-read platforms running at massive scale and continues to dominate the sequencing market by revenue.
Illumina has also been developing its own long-read capability, called Complete Long Reads, which uses a clever short-read-based proxy to derive long-range information.
The success of that approach versus true single-molecule long reads remains to be seen.
And of course, the FTC blocked Illumina from buying PacBio outright, so the two are head-to-head competitors in the long-read space.
The deeper conclusion, looking at the platform's full trajectory.
PacBio was the first credible attempt at single-molecule sequencing, and it succeeded after a long and difficult execution.
The technology was sound from the beginning, but the chemistry, the polymerase, the optics, the chip, and the workflow all had to mature for the platform to deliver on its promise.
The HiFi paradigm shift in 2019-2020 was the moment everything clicked into place.

Today, PacBio HiFi is the gold standard for high-accuracy long-read sequencing, the platform that finished the human genome, the platform behind the Vertebrate Genomes Project, and the platform increasingly used in clinical rare disease work.
If you walk out of here remembering one big idea, let it be this.

PacBio solved the long-read accuracy problem by reading the same single molecule many times — using the SMRTbell to fold the molecule into a loop, the phi29 polymerase to chug around the loop without falling off, and the CCS algorithm to average across passes — turning fifteen percent raw error into Q30-plus consensus quality without ever amplifying the DNA.
That's the platform.
That's why it matters.
That's why the genome got finished, finally, twenty years after the draft.
Alright.
That's PacBio.
Let's spend a few minutes on the appendix — some loose ends, some practical points, and then we'll open up for questions.
